reasoning:
The 6-month Treasury yield (DGS6MO) has been in a clear downtrend over the past year, falling from 4.46% in October 2024 to 3.76% by October 24, 2025. This reflects an ongoing Federal Reserve rate-cutting cycle. Over very short horizons (e.g., 5-7 trading days), yields exhibit significant daily volatility but have a negative drift. Daily changes are typically small (1-3 bps), meaning a negative drift of even ~0.3 bps/day pushes the probability of an increase below 50% (estimated at ~35-40% for the first resolution date).

As the forecast horizon extends to 1 month and 3 months, uncertainty regarding future macroeconomic data, Fed policy expectations, and possible reversals increases substantially. In line with the instruction that uncertainty should increase with forecast horizon (driving probabilities toward 0.5), I have adjusted the longer-term probabilities closer to 50/50. Thus, I assign 38% for the immediate 1-week horizon, 43% for the 1-month horizon, and 47% for the 3-month horizon, reflecting the strong near-term downward momentum tempered by long-term uncertainty.
